one_liner: A 24-year-old man presents with 9 months of progressive low back pain and stiffness that is worst in the morning and improves when he plays soccer.

stage2.hpi_extension: The pain is insidious, centered over the sacroiliac joints and lumbar spine, and has been slowly worsening over many months. Morning stiffness lasts more than one hour and significantly improves with activity but worsens with prolonged rest. He has been woken from sleep in the second half of the night by the back pain. Over the past 3 months he has also noticed that taking a deep breath feels restricted. Two months ago he had a painful red right eye with photophobia that resolved over a week.
stage2.pmh: Episode of anterior uveitis 2 months ago, otherwise healthy
stage2.family_history: Father has chronic back problems and an unspecified 'spine disease'
stage2.social_history: Graduate student. Plays recreational soccer. Non-smoker. No IV drug use.
stage2.physical_exam: General: well-appearing young man standing with mildly stooped posture. MSK: tenderness over bilateral sacroiliac joints; positive FABER (Patrick) test bilaterally. Decreased lumbar forward flexion on Schober test (<5 cm increase). Reduced chest expansion (<2.5 cm at the 4th intercostal space). Hips with reduced internal rotation. Eyes: mild residual conjunctival injection on the right. Cardiac: soft early diastolic murmur at the left sternal border suggestive of aortic regurgitation. Lungs clear. Skin: no rash, no psoriatic plaques, no nail changes.

lab_results.result: ESR 52 mm/hr (elevated), CRP 28 mg/L (elevated). CBC with mild normocytic anemia of chronic disease.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Elevated inflammatory markers support an active systemic inflammatory arthropathy and help distinguish inflammatory back pain from mechanical back pain.

lab_results.result: AP pelvis X-ray: bilateral symmetric sacroiliitis with joint space narrowing, erosions, and sclerosis. Lumbar spine X-ray: squaring of vertebral bodies with bridging syndesmophytes producing a 'bamboo spine' appearance. No compression fractures.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Bilateral sacroiliitis is the radiographic hallmark of ankylosing spondylitis; bamboo spine reflects syndesmophyte-mediated vertebral fusion.
HLA-B27 is strongly associated with ankylosing spondylitis and the broader seronegative spondyloarthropathies; it supports the diagnosis in the right clinical context.

stage5.final_clue: NSAIDs are started with symptomatic improvement. Because of persistent disease activity and limited range of motion, a TNF-alpha inhibitor (etanercept) is initiated, producing substantial relief of back stiffness and normalization of CRP at 3 months.

debrief.classic_presentation: Ankylosing spondylitis classically presents in young men (15-40 years) with insidious onset of chronic inflammatory low back pain and morning stiffness lasting more than one hour that improves with exercise and worsens with rest. Progressive spinal fusion leads to loss of lumbar lordosis, thoracic kyphosis, and decreased chest expansion. Extra-articular manifestations include anterior uveitis (most common), aortic regurgitation, apical pulmonary fibrosis, and cauda equina syndrome from dural ectasia.
debrief.pathophysiology: AS is a seronegative spondyloarthropathy strongly linked to HLA-B27 (>90% of patients). Enthesitis  -  inflammation at sites where tendons and ligaments insert into bone  -  is the core lesion. Chronic inflammation at the sacroiliac joints and vertebral corners leads to reactive new bone formation, producing syndesmophytes that bridge adjacent vertebrae and culminate in the radiographic bamboo spine. The disease is 'seronegative' because rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP are negative.
debrief.treatment_overview: First-line therapy is NSAIDs (indomethacin, naproxen), which provide symptomatic relief and may slow radiographic progression. Physical therapy and postural exercises are essential to preserve spinal mobility. For persistent active disease despite NSAIDs, TNF-alpha inhibitors (etanercept, infliximab, adalimumab) are highly effective. IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab) are an alternative. Conventional DMARDs like methotrexate and sulfasalazine are useful primarily for peripheral joint disease but not for axial disease.
high_yield_pearls: Inflammatory back pain: insidious onset before age 40, morning stiffness >1 hour, improves with exercise, worsens with rest, pain at night.
Bilateral symmetric sacroiliitis on pelvic X-ray is the earliest and most characteristic radiographic finding.
HLA-B27 is positive in >90% of ankylosing spondylitis patients; the B27 family of spondyloarthropathies is remembered as PAIR: Psoriatic, Ankylosing spondylitis, IBD-associated, Reactive arthritis.
Extra-articular features: anterior uveitis (most common), aortic regurgitation, apical pulmonary fibrosis, restrictive lung disease from decreased chest expansion.
Bamboo spine increases fracture risk even with minor trauma; any AS patient with new back pain after a fall needs urgent imaging for cervical spine fracture.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017)  -  Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Seronegative spondyloarthropathies  -  Ankylosing spondylitis' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017)  -  Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'HLA-B27 (PAIR) associations'.
